acceptedAnswer.text: Public trust: created for the benefit of the public at large (charitable, religious, educational). Private trust: created for specific beneficiaries (family members). Public trusts are eligible for 12A/80G tax exemptions.

mainEntity.name: What is needed for trust registration?

acceptedAnswer.text: Trust deed (on stamp paper), settlor and trustees details, trust property details, objects of the trust. Registration at the Sub-Registrar office of the jurisdiction where the property is located.

mainEntity.name: What types of trusts exist in India?

acceptedAnswer.text: Public charitable trust (for public benefit), private trust (for specific persons/family), and religious trust. Public trusts governed by state Trust Acts (Maharashtra, Gujarat have specific Acts). Other states: Indian Trusts Act, 1882.
Minimum 2 trustees recommended (no legal minimum for private trusts). No maximum. Author of trust (settlor) can also be trustee. Trust deed specifies governance structure.

Registration of trust deed is compulsory under Indian Registration Act, 1908 (if immovable property involved). Even without property, registration recommended for legal recognition and tax exemption eligibility.

mainEntity.name: Can a trust receive foreign donations?

acceptedAnswer.text: Only with FCRA registration from Ministry of Home Affairs. FCRA trust must have 3 years of track record of charitable activities. Separate designated bank account for FCRA funds.

mainEntity.name: What tax benefits does a trust get?

acceptedAnswer.text: Section 12A/12AB registration: income exempt from tax if 85% applied to objects. Section 80G: donors get 50% or 100% deduction. Must file ITR-7 annually. Must not have commercial activity exceeding threshold.

Public trust: created for the benefit of the public at large (charitable, religious, educational). Private trust: created for specific beneficiaries (family members). Public trusts are eligible for 12A/80G tax exemptions.
